title: Bungwe latsopano lotchedwa Federation of Civil Service Unions
body: Mabungwe akuluakulu a ogwira ntchito m’boma akana kuti bungwe la Teachers Union of Malawi (TUM) ndi Civil Servants Trade Union (CSTU) ndilo lowayimilira, ndipo akhazikitsa bungwe latsopano lotchedwa Federation of Civil Service Unions (FOCSU). M’chikalata chogwirizana, mabungwe a Secondary School Teachers Union (SESTU), Teacher Educators Union (TEDU), Medical Doctors Union of Malawi (MDUM), Physician Associates Union of Malawi (PAUM), Human Resources for Health Coalition (HRHC), Concerned Teachers of Malawi (CTM) ndi Concerned Civil Servants (CCS) ati TUM ndi CSTU alephera kufunsa mamembala awo pa nkhani ya kusintha kwa malipiro ndi kagwiridwe ka  ntchito. Potengera lamulo la Labour Relations Act la chaka cha 1996, mabungwewa ati ogwira ntchito ndiwo okha ali ndi ufulu wosankha amene angawaimire. M’chikalatacho, madandaulo akuluakulu akuphatikizapo kuwonjezeredwa kwa malipiro kwa 10 mpaka 20 peresenti komwe akuti sikugwirizana ndi mavuto a chuma omwe alipo pano; ndalama ya tchuthi ya 25 peresenti yomwe akuti ndi yochepa kwambiri poyerekeza ndi kukwera kwa mitengo ya mafuta; komanso kusasintha kwa ndalama zoyendera ndi zina zapadera ngakhale kuti mtengo wa moyo ukukwera kwambiri. Chikalatacho chadzudzulanso kugula kwa hotelo ya Amaryllis ndi Public Service Pension Trust Fund, kuphatikizapo kulipira ndalama zokwana K90 biliyoni kwa Yusuf Investments ngakhale kuti Reserve Bank inalamula kuti malondawo ayimitsidwe, komanso kuchotsedwa kwa K5 biliyoni ku ndalama zomwe zinali zoletsedwa popanda kuyankhapo kwa mabungwe. Kuphatikiza apo, mabungwewa akuimba mlandu boma kuti laphwanya lamulo la Pensions Act la 2023 posunga ndalama zokwana K4 biliyoni za penshoni kwa zaka ziwiri. Bungwe latsopanoli lati likuyembekezeka kuchita mkumano wogwirizana ku Lilongwe mkati mwa sabata ino kuti likambirane njira zomwe lingatsatire. Wolemba: Mussa Kabichi-Mangochi|20/04/26

title: STRONG TURNOUT MARKS OPENING OF TERM 3 IN MALAWI SCHOOLS
body: By Mussa Kabichi | Schools across Malawi have today, 20 April 2026, reopened for Term 3 of the 2025–2026 academic session, with reports from across the country indicating an encouraging turnout of learners on the very first day. In Mulanje District, particularly in Namphungo Zone, schools registered impressive attendance figures, with learners reporting in large numbers. Education authorities attribute the development to the impact of Free Primary Education (FPE) and increased community awareness. Messages shared in churches and mosques ahead of the opening day also played a key role in reminding parents and guardians of the school calendar. A teacher in the district, Chrisprin Chataika, welcomed the turnout, saying, “The response from learners is very encouraging. With Free Primary Education in place, there is no reason for any child to stay at home. We are ready to guide them to succeed this term.” In Balaka District, similar scenes were observed in M’Manga Zone, Nkhonde Zone, and Namalomba Zone, where schools also recorded high attendance. Community leaders and education stakeholders have been actively encouraging learners to return to school, especially as this is the final term of the academic year. A community member, Memory Lapken, emphasized the importance of education, stating, “As a community, we are making sure no child is left behind. With school fees removed, every learner has the opportunity to attend, and we are encouraging parents to take this seriously.” Meanwhile, in the Mangochi District, reports also indicate a strong turnout in several schools, with learners showing determination to perform well and advance to the next class. Many schools began the day with cleaning activities and orientation sessions before lessons resumed. A Parent-Teacher Association (PTA) member, Magret Injesi, expressed optimism about the term, saying, “We are impressed with how learners have reported in large numbers. This shows that both parents and learners understand the value of education, especially in this final term.

title: Free Education Rollout Receives Mixed Reactions in Malawi
body:
By Mussa Kabichi | 16 March 2026

The government’s rollout of free education in public primary and secondary schools is receiving mixed reactions, with stakeholders welcoming increased access while raising concerns over funding and implementation.

The policy, introduced this term, has eased the financial burden on many households by removing school-related costs such as fees and contributions. At the secondary level, learners selected to government schools can now attend without paying tuition, a development widely praised by parents.

However, findings by Education Matters Online show that while the initiative has boosted enrolment, schools are struggling to cope with limited and delayed funding under the Free Primary Education (FPE) program.

Headteachers and teachers say the current allocation of about K1,000 per learner is insufficient to meet operational demands, particularly for examinations, which in some cases cost up to K2,000 per learner. They also report that funds are often disbursed late and in small portions, affecting the planning and delivery of lessons.

“FPE is a good initiative, but the funding is too little and does not come on time,” said one teacher. Others noted that some schools are failing to print examination papers due to financial constraints, forcing learners to write tests from chalkboards.

Despite these challenges, the policy has led to a noticeable increase in learner participation. Teachers report a rise in the number of pupils sitting for examinations, while many learners say they no longer miss exams due to lack of fees.

“We are happy that we can now sit for exams without paying,” said one learner, though others expressed concern over the difficulty of writing exams from the board due to limited resources.

School committees and other stakeholders have also called for increased and timely funding, emphasizing the need for clear guidelines on resource allocation and management to ensure smooth implementation.

Parents, on the other hand, have largely welcomed the initiative, describing it as a major relief. Many say their children are now able to attend school consistently without the risk of being sent home for non-payment.

While the policy marks a significant step toward expanding access to education, stakeholders say its success will depend on addressing funding gaps and improving implementation. Strengthening financial support and ensuring timely disbursement of resources will be critical to delivering not only access but also quality education across Malawi.

title: Mangochi Education Director Attends Funeral of Primary School Learner
body:
By Mussa Kabichi | March 7, 2026

Director of Education and Sports for Mangochi District, MacDonald Glyn Kuse, is attending the funeral ceremony of Precious Smart, a Standard 7 learner at Mufti Abbas Primary School in Boma Zone.

Precious died on Thursday at Mangochi District Hospital following a short illness, according to sources close to the family.

The funeral ceremony is being held at Issa Mponda Village (Nansenga), located about 20 kilometres from Mangochi Town, where mourners including family members, fellow learners, teachers, and community members have gathered to pay their last respects.

Kuse’s presence at the funeral reflects the education office’s solidarity with the bereaved family and the school community during this period of mourning.

The passing of the learner has left a somber mood among pupils and staff at Mufti Abbas Primary School, highlighting the profound impact of the loss on the school and the wider community.

title: Mangochi Hosts High-Level BeFIT Review Meeting as Stakeholders Chart Way Forward for Education Innovation
author: Mussa Kabichi
body:
Education stakeholders in Mangochi District convened for a BeFIT end-of-Term 2 review meeting, focusing on assessing progress, addressing implementation gaps, and strengthening strategies for the next phase of the program. The meeting provided a platform for participants to share field experiences, identify key challenges, and propose practical interventions.

The meeting was attended by Director of Education and Sports MacDonald Glyn Kuse, Chief Education Officer Ruth Chirwa, Coordinating Primary Education Advisor Bassanio Kachere, members of the District Implementation Team, representatives of Zonal Implementation Teams, and headteachers.

Opening the session, MacDonald Glyn Kuse called on ZIT and School Implementation Team to safeguard BeFIT learning materials, particularly as Edukans prepares to hand over the programme to the Ministry of Education. He stressed the need to involve senior class teachers in BeFIT sessions to ensure wider understanding, consistency, and ownership.

Challenges highlighted included limited monitoring by Zonal Implementation Teams, inadequate supply of tablets, damage to equipment, adverse weather disruptions, insufficient solar power in some schools, and misuse of BeFIT equipment.

Despite setbacks, participants reported progress: improved Time on Task, an open day at Chiponde, strengthened community engagement through ADC meetings, Leadership Refresher trainings, training of 558 teachers across 29 schools, and increased inclusion of learners with diverse needs.

Edukans District Supervisor Richard Chiwaula commended Zonal Implementation Teams for intensifying school monitoring, noting improved program delivery and accountability.

Strategies for Term 3 were outlined to enhance efficiency and sustain progress. A total of 12 education zones participated, with six zones meeting at Boma TDC and six at Chiponde TDC. Currently, BeFIT is implemented in 29 schools across 12 zones, benefiting over 31,893 learners.

By 2030, the Government of Malawi aims to roll out BeFIT nationwide to all public primary schools.

The BeFIT Program, led by the Government of Malawi, strengthens foundational literacy and numeracy for Standards 1 to 4 learners using specialized Android tablet applications for personalized, self-paced learning.

title: Mangochi Education Director Engages Teachers in Boma Zone Tour
author: Mussa Kabichi
body:
Teachers in Boma Zone, Mangochi District, are engaging directly with district education authorities as Director of Education and Sports, MacDonald Glyn Kuse, continues his familiarisation tour.

Kuse visited Mchoka and Mufti Abbas Primary Schools alongside senior officials from the District Education Office, including Principal Education Officer Solomon Bonde, Coordinating Primary Education Advisor Bassanio Kachere, Head of Human Resources Blessings Gangire, and Boma Zone PEA Mariam Sambo.

During the visits, Kuse commended teachers for their performance and urged them to remain committed to their professional duties. He also held discussions with staff to understand challenges affecting their work.

Responding to concerns, Kuse said the district is working to address key issues, including plans to construct teachers’ houses within school premises and improve sanitation facilities.

On Free Primary Education (FPE), he indicated that the government is expected to increase funding in the 2026/2027 financial year to support teaching and learning materials, as well as other school operations.

The tour underscores the district’s commitment to strengthening collaboration with teachers, addressing workplace challenges, and improving the overall quality of education in Mangochi.
